title: Proposal
text: GLP-1 receptor signaling may regulate downstream genes involved in the cAMP/PKA pathway. In this project, we analyze publicly available data to evaluate whether tissues with higher GLP-1R expression show greater downstream pathway gene activity and whether treatment is associated with increased expression of GLP-1R and its downstream signalling genes compared with untreated samples.

title: Proposal
text: GLP-1 receptor signaling may regulate downstream genes involved in the cAMP/PKA pathway. In this project, we analyze publicly available data to evaluate whether tissues with higher GLP-1R expression show greater downstream pathway gene activity and whether treatment is associated with increased expression of GLP-1R and its downstream signalling genes compared with untreated samples.

title: Proposal
text:
Dataset(s)

Source: NCBI GEO Public Dataset (GSE293291)
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/geo/query/acc.cgi?acc=GSE293291
Sample size: 60 samples
Key variables:

Tissue type
Genotype
Treatment type
Gene expression measurements

License/usage: The dataset is publicly available through GEO and includes downloadable processed expression files as well as raw data in SRA. The GEO accession page does not list a separate explicit reuse license, so we cite the GEO accession page when using the data.

title: Proposal
text:
Planned analysis
Describe your plan in bullets:

Data cleaning and preprocessing

Combine expression files into one dataset
Match metadata to expression matrix (GSM IDs)
Log2 transformation and filtering of low-expression genes
Remove zero-variance genes before analysis

Cleaning/QC

Perform PCA to identify clustering and outliers
Evaluate variance explained by principal components
Check separation by treatment and genotype

Visualizations

PCA plots for QC
Volcano plots for differential expression
Heatmaps of top differentially expressed genes

Statistical/ML approach

Build design matrix based on genotype, treatment, and tissues
Fit linear model using limma
Identify differentially expressed genes

Validation & Intrepretation

Compare treated vs untreated groups

Peptide Treatment: mGIPR-Ab/P1

Vehicle Treatment

Focus on balanced comaprisons
Interpret results in context of GLP-1R and cAMP/PKA pathway.

title: Proposal
text:
Deliverables

Website sections

PCA Plot for QC
Volcano Plot

Differential Expression Results Table

Any optional stretch goals

title: GLP-1R Expression and cAMP/PKA Signaling
text:
BNFO 420 Capstone — VCU
Investigating how GLP-1 receptor activation affects downstream gene expression across liver and fat tissue in obese mice using bulk RNA-seq analysis.

title: GLP-1R Expression and cAMP/PKA Signaling
text:
What We Did
We analyzed a publicly available bulk RNA-seq dataset from mice treated with mGIPR-Ab/P1, a dual-acting GLP-1R/GIPR agonist. Our goal was to test whether GLP-1R activation upregulates downstream cAMP/PKA pathway genes across liver and inguinal white adipose tissue (iWAT).

title: GLP-1R Expression and cAMP/PKA Signaling
text:
Key Findings

Drug effect was real but subtle — treated and vehicle samples separated clearly in PCA, but cAMP/PKA pathway genes did not reach strong significance.


Fat tissue responded more strongly than liver — iWAT showed more differentially expressed genes with treatment than liver did.


GIPR knockout had the largest effect — unexpectedly, deleting GIPR from CNS neurons caused more gene expression changes than either drug treatment or GLP-1R deletion.

title: Team
text:
Analysis Pipeline Overview
Our team built a complete RNA-seq analysis pipeline from scratch:

Data Acquisition (Mariya) — Downloaded and organized 60 samples from GEO accession GSE293291
Metadata Organization (Khadeeja) — Created sample annotation table linking files to experimental conditions
Normalization & Filtering (Zoya) — Quality control and gene filtering (54,343 → 14,152 genes)
Exploratory Analysis (Mariya) — PCA plots to validate data quality and sample clustering
Differential Expression (Khadeeja) — Limma analysis across four experimental comparisons
Visualization (Courtney & Mariya) — Volcano plots and heatmaps of key findings
Interpretation & Communication (Courtney & Saja) — Scientific synthesis and presentation design
Website Development (Zoya) — Project documentation and results dissemination

title: Results
text: We evaluated gene expression changes across liver and adipose tissue to understand GLP-1R and GIPR signaling effects. Our analysis revealed tissue-specific responses, unexpected GIPR dominance, and modest cAMP/PKA pathway activation.

title: Results
text: We evaluated gene expression changes across liver and adipose tissue to understand GLP-1R and GIPR signaling effects. Our analysis revealed tissue-specific responses, unexpected GIPR dominance, and modest cAMP/PKA pathway activation.

title: Results
text:
Gene Expression Filtering
Before conducting differential expression analysis, we filtered out low-expression genes to improve statistical reliability and reduce noise.




Figure 1. Gene expression filtering summary. Approximately 74% of genes were removed during quality control, retaining 14,152 genes in liver tissue and 14,715 genes in adipose tissue. Only genes with expression > 1 in at least 5 samples were retained for downstream analysis.



The filtering step removed ~40,000 low-expression genes, keeping only those with robust, detectable expression. This improves the reliability of differential expression testing and reduces false discovery rates.

title: Results
text:
Principal Component Analysis
We performed PCA to validate data quality and assess whether treatment drove global gene expression patterns.

Liver Tissue




Figure 2. PCA plot of liver GLP1R cohort. Each point represents one biological sample. Treated samples (red) separate clearly from vehicle controls (blue) along PC1 (26.4% variance) and PC2 (15.3% variance). The clean separation validates dataset quality before differential expression analysis.



Treatment samples cluster tightly in the positive PC1 space, while vehicle controls occupy the negative space. No obvious outliers are present, confirming consistent biological replicates.


Adipose Tissue (iWAT)




Figure 3. PCA plot of iWAT GLP1R cohort. Separation appears more pronounced than in liver tissue, with PC1 capturing 39.6% of variance and PC2 capturing 19.7%. This suggests a stronger treatment effect in fat tissue compared to liver.



The increased variance explained by PC1 in adipose tissue (39.6% vs 26.4% in liver) indicates that treatment has a more dominant effect on global gene expression in this tissue.

title: Results
text:
Differential Expression Analysis
We performed four differential expression comparisons using the Limma package to identify genes significantly altered by treatment or receptor knockout.

Liver: Drug Treatment Effect




Figure 4. Volcano plot comparing drug vs vehicle in liver tissue. Each point represents a gene. Red points indicate significantly upregulated genes (8 total), blue points show downregulated genes (49 total). Purple diamonds mark hypothesis genes (GLP1R, PRKACA, PRKACB, etc.) from the cAMP/PKA pathway. Significance threshold: FDR < 0.05, |log2FC| > 1.



Key findings:

57 differentially expressed genes (8 up, 49 down)
Modest overall response — most genes cluster near zero fold change
cAMP/PKA pathway genes not significant — hypothesis genes (purple diamonds) fall below the significance threshold
Interpretation: Liver shows weak transcriptional activation in response to GLP-1 agonist treatment




Liver: GLP-1R Knockout vs Control




Figure 5. Volcano plot comparing GLP-1R knockout vs control in liver. CNS-specific deletion of the GLP-1 receptor caused widespread gene expression changes. 87 genes were upregulated and 173 downregulated (260 total). cAMP/PKA genes show borderline significance.



Key findings:

260 differentially expressed genes (87 up, 173 down)
Stronger effect than drug treatment — removing the receptor causes more changes than activating it
cAMP/PKA genes at borderline significance — suggesting pathway involvement when receptor is absent
Interpretation: GLP-1R deletion has system-level effects beyond direct receptor activation




Liver: GIPR Knockout vs Control




Figure 6. Volcano plot comparing GIPR knockout vs control in liver. CNS-specific deletion of the GIP receptor produced the most dramatic transcriptional changes of any comparison, with 299 upregulated and 161 downregulated genes (460 total).



Key findings:

460 differentially expressed genes (299 up, 161 down) — largest effect observed
Strong global transcriptional impact — nearly double the GLP-1R knockout effect
Hypothesis genes remain non-significant — GIPR affects genes outside our predicted pathway
Interpretation: GIPR plays an unexpectedly major regulatory role in liver gene expression through CNS-mediated pathways




Adipose Tissue: Drug Treatment Effect




Figure 7. Volcano plot comparing drug vs vehicle in adipose tissue (iWAT). Fat tissue showed a stronger response than liver to the same GLP-1 agonist treatment, with 241 differentially expressed genes (49 up, 192 down).



Key findings:

241 differentially expressed genes (49 up, 192 down)
Stronger response than liver — 4-fold more genes affected compared to liver treatment
cAMP/PKA genes near significance — closer to threshold than in liver
Interpretation: GLP-1 signaling is tissue-specific, with adipose tissue showing enhanced sensitivity

title: Results
text:
Gene Expression Patterns
Heatmaps reveal coordinated expression changes across the top differentially expressed genes.

Top 10 Differentially Expressed Genes




Figure 8. Heatmap of top 10 differentially expressed genes. Treatment samples (right columns) show consistently higher expression (red) compared to vehicle controls (left columns, blue). Genes include Hnmpl, Serbp1, Rpl8, and others. Clear clustering by condition demonstrates robust, reproducible treatment effects.



The dendrogram shows perfect separation between treatment and vehicle groups, confirming that the top differentially expressed genes are consistent across biological replicates.


Top 3 Differentially Expressed Genes




Figure 9. Heatmap of top 3 most significant genes (Hnmpl, Serbp1, Rpl8). Zoomed view reinforces the pattern: treatment consistently increases expression of these genes across all samples. The effect is coordinated and reproducible.

title: Results
text:
Summary of Findings
Our comprehensive analysis across multiple comparisons revealed:






NoteKey Takeaways




Data Quality Validated — PCA plots confirmed clean clustering by treatment with no outliers
Modest Liver Treatment Effect — Only 57 genes changed with drug treatment in liver
Tissue-Specific Responses — Adipose tissue (241 genes) responded 4× more strongly than liver (57 genes)
Receptor Knockout > Drug Treatment — Deleting receptors caused more dramatic changes than activating them
GIPR Dominance — GIPR knockout produced the largest transcriptional response (460 genes)
cAMP/PKA Pathway Subtle — Hypothesis genes showed trends but did not reach statistical significance



These results suggest that GLP-1R signaling operates through tissue-specific mechanisms, with GIPR playing an unexpectedly central role in CNS-mediated regulation of peripheral metabolism. The modest cAMP/PKA pathway activation indicates that GLP-1 drugs may work through alternative or redundant signaling cascades.

title: Results
section: Biological Interpretation: What the Top Genes Tell Us
text:
Biological Interpretation: What the Top Genes Tell Us
While our hypothesis focused on cAMP/PKA pathway genes, the most significantly changed genes reveal important insights into how GLP-1 treatment actually remodels cellular metabolism.

Understanding the Top Differentially Expressed Genes
The heatmaps highlight genes with the strongest, most consistent changes across all samples. Rather than seeing our predicted cAMP/PKA genes at the top, we found genes involved in fundamental metabolic processes:
Protein Synthesis Machinery (Rpl8, Rpl18a, Fbl, Emg1)
Multiple ribosomal proteins were upregulated, indicating that treated cells are ramping up their capacity to make new proteins. This suggests GLP-1 triggers broad metabolic reprogramming that requires building new cellular machinery rather than just activating existing pathways.
Metabolic Master Regulators (Hnmpl)
HNF1 homeobox-like protein functions as a master switch controlling liver-specific metabolic gene networks. Its appearance as a top hit indicates GLP-1 works through hierarchical regulatory networks—activating master regulators that coordinate expression of many downstream genes simultaneously.
RNA Processing & Translation Control (Serbp1)
SERPINE1 mRNA binding protein regulates which messenger RNAs get translated into proteins. Changes in this gene suggest GLP-1 affects not just gene transcription, but also post-transcriptional control of which proteins actually get made.
Lipid Metabolism (Pex6)
Peroxisomal biogenesis factor 6 builds the organelles responsible for breaking down fatty acids. Its upregulation directly connects to the weight loss effects of GLP-1 drugs by increasing cellular fat-burning capacity.


Why cAMP/PKA Genes Weren’t Significant
This apparent discrepancy actually reveals an important biological principle:
The cAMP/PKA pathway primarily works through rapid protein modification, not transcriptional changes. When GLP-1 binds its receptor:

Immediate effect (seconds-minutes): cAMP rises, activating PKA proteins that are already present in the cell
PKA phosphorylates existing proteins — changing their activity without requiring new gene expression
Downstream transcriptional changes (hours): Activated proteins then regulate other genes

Our RNA-seq experiment captures step 3 — the broad metabolic remodeling orchestrated by activated cAMP/PKA proteins, not the initial pathway activation itself. This explains why we see modest changes in PRKACA and PRKACB (the genes encoding PKA) but dramatic changes in their downstream targets like ribosomal proteins and metabolic regulators.


Connecting Molecular Findings to Clinical Effects
These gene expression patterns help explain the therapeutic benefits of GLP-1 drugs:

Increased ribosomal proteins → Enhanced capacity for producing metabolic enzymes and receptors
Activation of metabolic master regulators → Coordinated reprogramming of glucose and lipid handling pathways
Upregulation of peroxisomal genes → Increased fat oxidation contributing to sustained weight loss
Tissue-specific responses → Adipose tissue shows 4× stronger transcriptional changes than liver, consistent with tissue-selective drug effects observed clinically



Revised Understanding of GLP-1 Mechanism
Our results suggest GLP-1 receptor agonists work not by simply activating one linear signaling pathway, but by triggering coordinated metabolic reprogramming across multiple regulatory networks. The combination of:

Subtle cAMP/PKA transcriptional changes (because the pathway works at the protein level)
Strong downstream effector gene changes (ribosomal proteins, metabolic regulators, lipid metabolism genes)
Tissue-specific response magnitudes (stronger in adipose than liver)

…indicates that the cAMP/PKA pathway functions as a signal amplifier — small initial molecular changes cascade into large-scale metabolic remodeling. This multi-level regulation may explain why GLP-1 drugs have such broad therapeutic effects on glucose control, weight loss, and cardiovascular health.


Implications for Future Research
These findings raise several important questions:

Protein-level validation: Do protein levels and phosphorylation states of cAMP/PKA pathway components change even when their gene expression doesn’t?
Time-course dynamics: How do these transcriptional changes evolve over hours to days after treatment?
Functional consequences: Do the observed changes in ribosomal and metabolic genes translate into measurable differences in protein synthesis rates and metabolic flux?
GIPR’s unexpected role: Why does GIPR knockout cause more dramatic transcriptional changes than GLP-1R knockout, and what does this mean for dual agonist drug development?

Our hypothesis was not disproven—it was refined. We learned that GLP-1 signaling works through layers of regulation, with rapid protein-level changes driving slower but more extensive transcriptional remodeling of cellular metabolism.

title: Introduction
text: GLP-1 receptor agonists like semaglutide (Ozempic, Wegovy) and tirzepatide (Mounjaro) have revolutionized treatment for type 2 diabetes and obesity. However, the molecular mechanisms underlying their therapeutic effects remain incompletely understood. This project investigates how GLP-1 receptor (GLP-1R) signaling affects gene expression in metabolically active tissues using RNA-seq data from mice treated with a dual GLP-1R/GIPR agonist (mGIPR-Ab/P1). Our analysis revealed tissue-specific responses, unexpected GIPR dominance in regulating peripheral gene expression, and modest transcriptional changes in the predicted cAMP/PKA pathway.

title: Introduction
text: GLP-1 receptor agonists like semaglutide (Ozempic, Wegovy) and tirzepatide (Mounjaro) have revolutionized treatment for type 2 diabetes and obesity. However, the molecular mechanisms underlying their therapeutic effects remain incompletely understood. This project investigates how GLP-1 receptor (GLP-1R) signaling affects gene expression in metabolically active tissues using RNA-seq data from mice treated with a dual GLP-1R/GIPR agonist (mGIPR-Ab/P1). Our analysis revealed tissue-specific responses, unexpected GIPR dominance in regulating peripheral gene expression, and modest transcriptional changes in the predicted cAMP/PKA pathway.

title: Introduction
text:
Background Knowledge on GLP-1
GLP-1 (glucagon-like peptide-1) is an incretin hormone secreted by intestinal L-cells in response to food intake. It plays a central role in glucose homeostasis and energy regulation by:

Enhancing insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells in a glucose-dependent manner
Suppressing glucagon release from alpha cells
Slowing gastric emptying to reduce post-meal glucose spikes
Promoting satiety through central nervous system pathways

The GLP-1 receptor is a G-protein coupled receptor (GPCR) expressed in multiple tissues including pancreas, brain, heart, kidney, liver, and adipose tissue. Upon ligand binding, GLP-1R activates the cAMP/PKA signaling pathway, which drives downstream transcriptional changes. This pathway includes key genes such as PRKACA, PRKACB, and CREB1 that regulate metabolic gene expression.

title: Introduction
text:
Gap in Knowledge
While GLP-1 drugs are clinically effective, several questions remain unanswered:

Which genes are directly regulated by GLP-1R signaling in peripheral metabolic tissues like liver and fat?
Does GLP-1R activation robustly upregulate the cAMP/PKA pathway genes at the transcriptional level, or does it work through post-translational mechanisms?
How does tissue-specific gene expression differ between liver and adipose tissue in response to GLP-1 treatment?
What is the relative contribution of GIPR (glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide receptor), which is often co-targeted with GLP-1R in dual agonist drugs?
Do receptor knockout models reveal compensatory mechanisms or confirm direct receptor-mediated transcriptional effects?

Understanding these mechanisms could inform the development of more targeted therapies and explain why dual GLP-1R/GIPR agonists show enhanced efficacy compared to single-receptor drugs.

title: Introduction
text:
What is GLP-1R?
GLP-1R (Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor) is a class B GPCR that mediates the physiological effects of GLP-1 hormone. Key characteristics include:

Tissue distribution: Pancreatic islets, brain (hypothalamus, brainstem), heart, liver, adipose tissue, kidney
Signaling mechanism: Primarily through Gαs protein → adenylyl cyclase → cAMP → PKA activation → CREB phosphorylation → gene transcription
Therapeutic targeting: GLP-1R agonists (exenatide, liraglutide, semaglutide) are FDA-approved for diabetes and weight management
CNS-mediated effects: Central GLP-1R activation contributes to appetite suppression and energy expenditure regulation

GIPR (Glucose-Dependent Insulinotropic Polypeptide Receptor) is a related incretin receptor that responds to GIP hormone. It shares similar downstream signaling pathways and tissue distribution with GLP-1R. Dual GLP-1R/GIPR agonists like tirzepatide show superior efficacy compared to GLP-1R-only drugs, suggesting complementary or synergistic mechanisms.
The mGIPR-Ab/P1 agonist used in this study is a dual agonist that activates both GLP-1R and GIPR, allowing us to assess combined incretin signaling effects.

title: Introduction
text:
Objective
This project aims to characterize the transcriptional response to GLP-1R signaling in liver and adipose tissue using published RNA-seq data from GEO (GSE293291). The dataset includes 60 samples across 4 genotypes, 2 treatment conditions, and 2 tissues.
Specific objectives include:

Test the hypothesis that GLP-1R activation increases expression of cAMP/PKA pathway genes (PRKACA, PRKACB, CREB1, etc.)
Compare treatment effects (mGIPR-Ab/P1 dual agonist drug) with receptor knockout models (CNS-specific GLP-1R knockout and GIPR knockout) to distinguish direct receptor-mediated effects
Evaluate tissue-specific responses between liver and inguinal white adipose tissue (iWAT)
Assess the relative importance of GIPR versus GLP-1R in regulating peripheral gene expression through CNS-mediated pathways
Identify differentially expressed genes that may contribute to the metabolic benefits of incretin-based therapies

title: Introduction
text:
Research Question and Hypothesis
Research Question:
Do cells that express GLP-1R show increased expression of downstream cAMP/PKA pathway genes in response to receptor activation?
Hypothesis:
We predicted that treatment with the mGIPR-Ab/P1 dual agonist would strongly upregulate GLP-1R expression and activate downstream cAMP/PKA pathway genes in both liver and adipose tissue, with similar effects across both tissues.
Independent Variable: Treatment condition (vehicle control vs. mGIPR-Ab/P1 dual agonist)
Dependent Variable: Expression levels of cAMP/PKA pathway genes and genome-wide gene expression patterns

title: Introduction
text:
Relevant Literature

Key Studies on Incretin Biology
Campbell, J. E., & Drucker, D. J. (2013). Pharmacology, physiology, and mechanisms of incretin hormone action. Cell Metabolism, 17(6), 819-837.
- Comprehensive review of GLP-1 and GIP signaling mechanisms, tissue distribution, and therapeutic applications
Müller, T. D., et al. (2019). Glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1). Molecular Metabolism, 30, 72-130.
- Detailed overview of GLP-1 biology, including CNS-mediated metabolic effects and receptor signaling pathways
Holst, J. J., & Rosenkilde, M. M. (2020). GIP as a therapeutic target in diabetes and obesity: Insight from incretin co-agonists. Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism, 105(8), e2710-e2716.
- Discusses the rationale for dual GLP-1R/GIPR agonists and the complementary roles of these receptors


Methodological References
Ritchie, M. E., et al. (2015). limma powers differential expression analyses for RNA-sequencing and microarray studies. Nucleic Acids Research, 43(7), e47.
- The statistical method (Limma) used for differential expression analysis in this project
Love, M. I., Huber, W., & Anders, S. (2014). Moderated estimation of fold change and dispersion for RNA-seq data with DESeq2. Genome Biology, 15(12), 550.
- Reference for normalization methods used in RNA-seq analysis


Dataset Source
Zhu, Z., et al. (2024). GSE293291: RNA-seq analysis of liver and adipose tissue from GLP-1R and GIPR knockout mice treated with mGIPR-Ab/P1. NCBI Gene Expression Omnibus.
- The published dataset used in this project, containing approximately 54,000 genes measured across 60 samples representing: - Genotypes: Wild-type (Glp1r fl/fl), CNS-specific GLP-1R knockout (Glp1r Wnt1-/-), CNS-specific GIPR knockout (Gipr Syn-/-) - Treatments: Vehicle control, mGIPR-Ab/P1 dual agonist - Tissues: Liver and inguinal white adipose tissue (iWAT)
